title: SFT Datasets
lesson:
SFT stands for Supervised Fine-Tuning. It is the most common way to teach an existing model a new skill or style. The word supervised just means "we show it the right answer."

You hand the model a pile of example pairs — a prompt and the response you wish it had written — and it practises copying that response. An SFT dataset is simply that pile of examples. This lesson is about what goes into the pile, because the dataset is the part you actually control, and it matters far more than most beginners expect.

The driving-lessons analogy

A raw pretrained model has read most of the internet (this is its pretraining, where it learned grammar, facts, and how words flow). But reading about driving is not the same as driving well.

SFT is like a learner driver sitting next to an instructor. The instructor says "here is the situation" (the prompt) and "here is exactly what a good driver does" (the response). The learner copies it, over and over, until the behaviour sticks.

Your dataset is the instructor. If your instructor demonstrates sloppy three-point turns, the model learns sloppy three-point turns. The model does not become smarter than your examples — it becomes like your examples.

What one example actually looks like

Each row in an SFT dataset is a conversation, usually stored as JSON. The standard shape today is a list of messages, each with a role and content:

{"messages": [
  {"role": "system", "content": "You are a concise support agent."},
  {"role": "user", "content": "My order hasn't arrived. Order 8842."},
  {"role": "assistant", "content": "Sorry about that! Order 8842 shipped Tuesday and is due Friday. Want me to email the tracking link?"}
]}

Files are usually saved as JSONL — JSON Lines, meaning one complete JSON object per line, so a million examples is just a million lines you can stream one at a time.

The key idea: during training, the model is graded only on the assistant text. The system and user messages are the question; the assistant message is the answer key it must learn to produce. Getting this exact wrapping right is its own topic — see Dataset Formatting.

Quality beats quantity, by a lot

A famous 2023 result (the "LIMA" study) showed that roughly 1,000 carefully hand-written examples could turn a base model into a genuinely helpful assistant — beating setups with 50x more messy data. The lesson stuck: in SFT, a small, clean, diverse dataset usually wins.

Think of it as the difference between studying 1,000 perfect worked examples versus 50,000 random homework sheets where half the answers are wrong. The wrong answers do not cancel out — the model dutifully learns them too.

So what makes examples good? Three things:

  • Correctness: the assistant response is actually right and well-written.
  • Diversity: the prompts cover the real range of things users will ask, not 800 variations of one question.
  • Consistency: the style, format, and tone are uniform, because the model averages everything it sees. If half your answers use bullet points and half use paragraphs, you are teaching it to flip a coin.

Where the examples come from

Three common sources, often mixed:

  • Human-written — slow, expensive, highest quality.
  • Collected from real logs — your actual support tickets or chat history, cleaned up.
  • Machine-generated using a stronger model — a fast-growing approach covered in Synthetic Datasets.

Most teams blend all three, then run heavy Data Curation and Dataset Cleaning before training a single step.

Pretraining dataSFT data
Raw text: books, web pages, codePrompt → ideal response pairs
Goal: learn language and factsGoal: learn how to behave on tasks
Trillions of tokensOften hundreds to tens of thousands of examples
No "right answer" — just predict next wordYou provide the right answer to copy

One more analogy to lock it in: pretraining fills the library with every book ever written. SFT is hiring a librarian and teaching them how to answer the questions people actually walk up and ask. The books were already there; the dataset teaches the helpful behaviour.

SFT is the foundation that later steps like Preference Datasets and DPO build on — you almost always do SFT first.

💡 Before scaling up, hand-write 50 examples yourself. You will discover what "good" even means for your task, and that becomes the rubric for everything you generate or collect later.
mentalModel: Your SFT dataset is a stack of flashcards: each card shows a prompt on the front and the ideal answer on the back. The model studies the cards until it answers like them — so it can only ever become as good, as varied, and as consistent as the cards you wrote.
takeaways: SFT teaches behaviour from prompt → ideal-response pairs the model copies.
Grade only the assistant message; the prompt is just context.
Quality, diversity, consistency beat raw size — clean small wins.
mistakes: Chasing volume over quality. Dumping in 100k scraped, half-wrong examples usually performs worse than 1,000 clean ones. The model copies your mistakes faithfully — it has no way to tell a good answer from a bad one.

Low diversity. Eight hundred near-identical prompts teach the model one narrow trick. Cover the real spread of tasks, phrasings, and edge cases your users will actually send, or it falls apart on anything slightly new.

Inconsistent style. Mixing terse and chatty, bulleted and prose, formal and casual answers across rows teaches the model to be erratic. Pick a target voice and format and keep every example faithful to it — see Instruction Tuning.

Training on the user text, not just the assistant text. If your loss mask is wrong, the model learns to predict the question instead of answering it. Only the assistant response should be graded. This is the single most common silent formatting bug — check Dataset Formatting.

Leaking your test set into training. If evaluation examples sneak into the training pile, your scores look great but mean nothing — the model just memorised the answers. De-duplicate and split before you start.

Expecting SFT to inject fresh facts reliably. SFT is brilliant for teaching behaviour, format, and tone, but it is a leaky way to store new knowledge. For up-to-date or large fact bases, reach for RAG instead of cramming facts into examples.
exercise:
Build a tiny SFT dataset by hand. Pick one narrow task — say, turning a messy meeting note into three clean action items. Write 10 examples in JSONL, each with a system, user, and assistant message, using the message shape from the lesson. Make the prompts genuinely different from one another (different topics, lengths, and tones), but keep the assistant output format identical every time (always exactly three bullet-point action items). Save it as train.jsonl and confirm each line parses as valid JSON on its own.

Stretch goal: Write 3 deliberately bad examples (wrong format, vague answers) and 3 great ones. Swap them in and out and predict which dataset would teach a cleaner model — then explain in one sentence why quantity could not save the bad set.
solution: Hint: A clean line looks like {"messages":[{"role":"system","content":"..."},{"role":"user","content":"..."},{"role":"assistant","content":"- ...\n- ...\n- ..."}]} with no trailing comma and the whole object on one line. To check each line, run python -c "import json,sys; [json.loads(l) for l in open('train.jsonl')]" — it errors on the first broken line. For the stretch goal: the bad set wins on nothing, because the model averages what it sees and copies sloppiness exactly. Adding more bad rows just teaches the bad behaviour more strongly. Ten consistent, correct examples beat fifty inconsistent ones — diversity in the prompts, consistency in the answers.
summary: An SFT (Supervised Fine-Tuning) dataset is a pile of prompt → ideal-response pairs the model practises copying. Store it as JSONL with role/content messages, and grade only the assistant text. Quality, diversity, and consistency matter far more than raw size — a small, clean, varied set usually beats a huge messy one, because the model learns to be exactly like its examples, mistakes included.

diagram.caption: In one SFT row the prompt is context, only the assistant answer is graded, and the model copies exactly what you feed it — so clean beats big.

quiz.q: During SFT training, which part of an example is the model actually graded on?
quiz.explain: The system and user messages are context (the question); the model is scored only on producing the assistant text, the answer key. Grading the prompt too is the classic loss-mask bug.

quiz.q: You have 1,000 clean, diverse, consistent examples and the option to add 50,000 scraped half-wrong ones. What usually gives the better model?
options: Neither — you need millions for SFT
quiz.explain: The LIMA-style finding is that a small clean set often beats a much larger messy one. The model copies wrong answers faithfully, so bad rows actively hurt rather than averaging out.

quiz.q: A teammate's fine-tuned support bot answers correctly but flips randomly between terse bullet lists and long chatty paragraphs. What is the most likely dataset cause?
quiz.explain: The model averages the styles it sees, so mixed formatting teaches it to flip a coin. Fix it by making the assistant answers uniform in voice and format, even while keeping prompts varied.

quiz.q: Your product needs the model to answer using your company's latest pricing, which changes monthly. Why is stuffing those facts into an SFT dataset a weak choice?
options: SFT cannot store any text at all
SFT is great for behaviour but a leaky, hard-to-update way to hold facts
SFT only works on code tasks
Facts must always go in the system message
quiz.explain: SFT excels at teaching behaviour, format, and tone, but baking in facts is unreliable and forces a retrain on every change. For fresh or large fact bases, retrieval (RAG) is the better fit.

keyTerms.def: Teaching a model by showing it prompt-and-ideal-response pairs and having it practise copying the responses.
A training setup where you provide the correct answer for each example, so the model has something exact to learn from.
The collection of prompt → ideal-response examples used for supervised fine-tuning.
JSON Lines: a file with one complete JSON object per line, so huge datasets can be read one example at a time.
The standard fields in a chat example. Role is who is speaking (system, user, or assistant); content is what they say.
The response the model is meant to produce. During training, only this text is graded — it is the answer key.
The setting that tells training which tokens to grade. For SFT it should cover only the assistant response, not the prompt.
How varied the prompts are. High diversity helps the model handle the real range of inputs instead of one narrow case.
When evaluation examples accidentally appear in the training set, making scores look great while meaning nothing.
